title: engine: CAP governor — 429-wall admission control above the worker pool
source: engine/docs/ENGINE-NOTES.md M1 gap table ('build it before any pool size >1 goes into real multi-ticket use')
prompt: Build the concurrency/429 governor for engine/orchestrator.py + engine/warm_pool.py. Three pieces: (1) HARD CAP: a CAP constant (default 3, per suborch's proven 'well under the ~5-7 per-account 429 wall') that clamps --pool at startup with a loud warning if the user asked for more. (2) LIMIT DETECTION: when a worker wedges (timeout path), sniff its tmux pane content (warm_pool has/gets a capture-pane helper) for usage-limit signatures ('usage limit', 'rate limit', '429', 'out of extended usage', case-insensitive); on detection, enter BACKOFF. (3) BACKOFF: write engine/store/BACKOFF flag-file ({ts, until, reason, worker}) with a cooldown (default 15 min); while BACKOFF is active and unexpired, orchestrator skips claim/dispatch exactly like the HALT gate (reuse that pattern, graceful, running tickets untouched) and status.py shows a BACKOFF banner; expired flag is auto-removed on next pass. Notify David via the existing notify() on entry. Keep it minimal and mirror the HALT flag-file idiom (docs/kill-switch-spec.md) so the two gates read identically. Update engine/docs/ENGINE-NOTES.md gap table row. Do NOT touch the halt logic itself.
verify: Unit-style: simulate by writing a BACKOFF flag manually -> orchestrator (a short --max-wall run with an empty queue is fine) logs the skip; status.py shows the banner; expired BACKOFF auto-clears. Pane-sniff: feed the detector function a captured string containing 'usage limit reached' -> returns True; ordinary pane text -> False. Capture transcript in ticket result.

result.verify_transcript: (a) looks_like_usage_limit(): '...usage limit reached...' -> True; 'rate limit exceeded' -> True; '429 Too Many Requests' -> True; 'out of extended usage' -> True; ordinary pane text ('Crafting response... esc to interrupt') -> False; '' / None -> False. (b) wrote a live BACKOFF flag via write_backoff() (until=now+2min): is_backoff()->True, backoff_info() returned the {ts,until,reason,worker} payload; replicated the exact main()-loop gate branch (is_halted() False -> elif is_backoff()) and confirmed it prints the "[backoff] ... skipping claim/dispatch this pass" log instead of proceeding to claim/dispatch. DID NOT boot the real orchestrator end-to-end: engine/store/queue/ had 1 live ticket and engine/store/running/ had 2 other in-flight sibling tickets at verify time -- an actual orchestrator.py run would have booted tmux workers and could have claimed/interfered with concurrent agents' work, so the gate was verified via a targeted call of the real is_halted()/is_backoff()/backoff_info() functions instead, per the ticket's own fallback guidance. (c) overwrote BACKOFF with an already-expired 'until' (now-1min): is_backoff() logged "[backoff]  cooldown expired (was until ...) -- auto-resuming claim/dispatch", removed the file, and returned False -- confirmed via os.path.exists() before/after. (d) status.py with flag present: human mode showed the "FACTORY IN BACKOFF (429 wall)" banner (since/until/reason/worker/flag lines) ABOVE the normal report; --json mode carried a populated "backoff" key ({ts,until,reason,worker}) alongside "halt": null. With the flag absent: human mode printed no banner (clean queue/running/etc. report only), --json mode carried "backoff": null. (e) --pool 5 clamp: did NOT invoke main() itself (same live-queue/sibling-agent safety concern as (b) -- a real run boots tmux workers). Instead replicated main()'s clamp block verbatim in a standalone script against the real orchestrator.CAP constant: requested pool_size=5 > CAP=3 -> printed the "[cap] *** requested --pool 5 exceeds hard CAP=3 ... clamping to 3 ***" warning and pool_size resolved to 3. This exercises the identical code text as main() but not main() itself executing end-to-end. (f) confirmed engine/store/BACKOFF absent after all tests (removed by the (c) auto-clear); py_compile confirms both orchestrator.py and status.py compile cleanly.
result.notes: CAP=3 clamp, looks_like_usage_limit() detector, write_backoff()/is_backoff()/backoff_info()/clear_backoff() (mirroring halt.py's idiom, with auto-expiry added), BACKOFF gate in the main loop alongside HALT, pane-sniff wired into the worker-timeout/reboot path (captured+tested BEFORE reboot), notify() on backoff entry, and a status.py BACKOFF banner + --json key all landed in orchestrator.py/status.py only -- did not touch warm_pool.py (WarmWorker.capture() already existed, reused as-is, no new helper needed) or halt.py (read-only, untouched). Honest gap: never exercised against a REAL usage-limit hit or a REAL live orchestrator run (both deferred for store/concurrency safety -- see transcript above); only synthetic pane text + a manually-written/expired flag + a verbatim logic replica were exercised.
result.orchestrator_review: 2026-07-06T08:23Z live run: orchestrator --pool 5 --max-wall 20 --teardown -> [cap] clamp 5->3 warning printed; 3 warm workers booted 10s; [backoff] skip logged with live flag; [skip] deferred ticket excluded; exit 0; pool torn down (tmux clean); BACKOFF untouched by run, cleared after. wake watcher: launchd loaded, DISARMED, debounce quiet-lines in wake.log, deferred ticket skipped.
